description:
You are the system administrator of a small fleet of web-servers.  
    For an internal audit you must generate two frequency reports from yesterday’s access log that lives at  

        /home/user/server_logs/access.log

    The reports must be produced only with standard GNU command-line utilities (e.g. sort, uniq, cut, awk, tee, etc.).  
    The goal is to demonstrate that you can build classic *sort | uniq* pipelines, possibly running in parallel.

    1.  Create the directory  

            /home/user/output

        if it does not already exist.

    2.  From /home/user/server_logs/access.log extract the client IPv4 address that appears at the very start of each line.  
        Count how many times each distinct IPv4 address occurs in the whole file.  
        a.  Write those counts to  

                /home/user/output/unique_ip_counts.txt

            in **exactly** the following machine-checkable format:
                • one record per line;  
                • the record must be:  `<IP_ADDRESS><single space><COUNT>` (no other delimiter, no extra spaces);  
                • sort the records primarily by descending COUNT and secondarily (for identical counts) by ascending IP address (lexicographical dotted-decimal order).  
            Only IPv4 addresses that are present in the log should appear – no header, no totals, no blank lines.

    3.  At the same time (you may use pipes, tee, subshells, etc.) produce a second frequency report that counts the HTTP status code that appears just after the closing quote of the request field in the log.  
        a.  Save the result to  

                /home/user/output/status_code_counts.txt

            with **exactly** this format:
                `<STATUS_CODE><single space><COUNT>` per line;  
                records sorted by descending COUNT and, for ties, ascending STATUS_CODE.  
            Again, no header, no extra spaces, no blank lines.

    4.  When everything is finished, list the two files to the console so the human reviewer can quickly see the outcome.

    Expected structure after you are done:

        /home/user/output/
        ├── unique_ip_counts.txt      # frequency table of IP addresses
        └── status_code_counts.txt    # frequency table of HTTP status codes

    Tips (NOT mandatory commands):  
        • You will probably start by cutting the first field for the IPs and another pipeline to isolate the status codes.  
        • Combining pipelines with ‘tee’ or background jobs will let you compute both tables without reading the log twice.  
        • Remember to sort before uniq ‑c, then a second sort for the required order.

    The automated checker will fail if:  
        • either output file is missing, misplaced, or has a different name;  
        • the ordering rules are not followed;  
        • there are extra or missing blank lines, headers, or delimiters;  
        • counts are inaccurate.

    Do everything solely from the terminal—no text editors or GUI tools.  Good luck!
